items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.correct: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (stroke volume x heart rate).
items.misconception: The volume of blood in the heart at any moment.
items.why: Students confuse stroke volume (one beat) with cardiac output (per minute total). CO = SV x HR. A 70mL stroke at 70bpm = 4.9L/min cardiac output.

items.q: Trait anxiety is best described as:
items.why: Correct answer: Stable personality disposition. “Momentary state” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

The answer examiners want is: Cognitive anxiety, somatic anxiety, self-confidence. “Trait anxiety only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If an athlete's CSAI-2 cognitive anxiety = 24, somatic = 18, confidence = 22, total scale points is?
items.why: The real figure is 64. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
